title: The Federal Reserve and Outlook for the Economy and Monetary Policy
summary: In Oklahoma City, Schmid argues the Fed is as close to its dual mandate as it has been in some time, with unemployment at 4.2% and a labor market in balance though cooling, and inflation at 2.6% still above target. He favors retaining a modestly restrictive stance and a patient approach to rate changes, reasoning that policy is only modestly restrictive and that with demand growth moderate this is exactly where it should be. He frames tariffs through a supply-demand-balance lens: keeping demand growth in check limits tariff passthrough, so the muted tariff effect is a sign policy is well calibrated rather than a reason to cut. He stresses he will remain data dependent and would adjust if demand weakens significantly.

key_quotes.quote: My own view, guided by the dual mandate, is that with the economy still showing momentum, growing business optimism, and inflation still stuck above our objective, retaining a modestly restrictive monetary policy stance remains appropriate for the time being.
key_quotes.context: His baseline policy recommendation in August.

key_quotes.quote: First, while monetary policy might currently be restrictive, it is not very restrictive. And second, given recent price pressures, a modestly restrictive stance is exactly where we want to be.
key_quotes.context: The two arguments behind his patient approach.

key_quotes.quote: Overall, I am anticipating a relatively muted effect of tariffs on inflation, but I view that as a sign that policy is appropriately calibrated rather than a sign that the policy rate should be cut.
key_quotes.context: On why muted tariff passthrough is not a reason to ease.

key_quotes.quote: By this comprehensive measure, the labor market, while cooling, remains in good health.
key_quotes.context: His read on the labor market using the KC Fed indicator.
